verses.text: Now the days of David drew nigh that he should die; and he charged Solomon his son, saying, I go the way of all the earth: be thou strong therefore, and shew thyself a man; And keep the charge of the LORD thy God, to walk in his ways, to keep his statutes, and his commandments, and his judgments, and his testimonies, as it is written in the law of Moses, that thou mayest prosper in all that thou doest, and whithersoever thou turnest thyself: That the LORD may continue his word which he spake concerning me, saying, If thy children take heed to their way, to walk before me in truth with all their heart and with all their soul, there shall not fail thee (said he) a man on the throne of Israel. Guidance: David's deathbed charge to Solomon — the general portion. The opening sentence is one of the most universal in Scripture: 'I go the way of all the earth.' Every human walks this same path; even great kings end here. David acknowledges his own mortality with dignity. Then the charge to Solomon. (1) 'Be thou strong therefore, and shew thyself a man' (chazaq ve-hayita le-ish — be strong and be a man). The same words Joshua heard from Moses (Deut 31:7), Joshua heard from the LORD (Josh 1:6-7); a generational transmission of courage-language from one Israelite leader to the next. (2) 'Keep the charge of the LORD thy God' — keep Torah. The charge that follows is the central content of any Israelite king's calling: walk in His ways, keep statutes (chuqqim), commandments (mitzvot), judgments (mishpatim), and testimonies (eduvot) — the full vocabulary of covenant fidelity, all explicitly tied to 'the law of Moses.' (3) The promise attached to obedience: 'that thou mayest prosper.' The Hebrew taskil — succeed, act with wisdom. (4) The dynastic promise: 'that the LORD may continue his word… If thy children take heed to their way… there shall not fail thee a man on the throne of Israel.' This is the Davidic covenant of 2 Sam 7 here applied with a moral condition. The eternal dynasty depends on faithfulness. The chapter is teaching us that even great promises of God have an obedience-component — not because God's grace is earned, but because God's blessings are received and walked in. The eternal Davidic king who will fulfill this perfectly is Christ. But the immediate Solomon is being told: keep covenant, and your line will continue. He will, tragically, fail at this; the latter half of his reign and his son Rehoboam will demonstrate how seriously the LORD takes the condition.

Moreover thou knowest also what Joab the son of Zeruiah did to me, and what he did to the two captains of the hosts of Israel, unto Abner the son of Ner, and unto Amasa the son of Jether, whom he slew, and shed the blood of war in peace, and put the blood of war upon his girdle that was about his loins, and in his shoes that were on his feet. Do therefore according to thy wisdom, and let not his hoar head go down to the grave in peace. But shew kindness unto the sons of Barzillai the Gileadite, and let them be of those that eat at thy table: for so they came to me when I fled because of Absalom thy brother. And, behold, thou hast with thee Shimei the son of Gera, a Benjamite of Bahurim, which cursed me with a grievous curse in the day when I went to Mahanaim: but he came down to meet me at Jordan, and I sware to him by the LORD, saying, I will not put thee to death with the sword. Now therefore hold him not guiltless: for thou art a wise man, and knowest what thou oughtest to do unto him; but his hoar head bring thou down to the grave with blood. Guidance: David's specific death-list. The transition from generalities to specifics is jarring — from 'keep Torah' to 'kill Joab.' The chapter is not afraid to record both sides of David's final wishes: spiritual heritage and political housekeeping. (1) Joab. David recites the case: Joab killed Abner (2 Sam 3) and Amasa (2 Sam 20) — both peace-time murders of commanders rivaling Joab. 'Shed the blood of war in peace, and put the blood of war upon his girdle… and in his shoes.' The image is concrete: Joab's belt and sandals carry the bloodstains of crimes that should never have happened. The blood is still legally on Joab and, by association, on David's house. The Mosaic principle is that unavenged blood pollutes the land (Num 35:33). David charges Solomon to settle the case. (2) The sons of Barzillai. The 80-year-old who supplied David's army during the Absalom rebellion (2 Sam 17:27-29; 19:31-39). David could not repay him in life; the debt passes to Solomon. 'Let them be of those that eat at thy table' — formal court hospitality, the way Mephibosheth was treated. The chapter is teaching us that righteous debts also pass between generations; what one generation receives, the next should repay. (3) Shimei. The Benjamite who cursed David during the Absalom rebellion (2 Sam 16). David swore not to kill him personally after the rebellion ended. But Shimei's curse remains unanswered. David passes the case to Solomon, who is not bound by David's personal oath. 'His hoar head bring thou down to the grave with blood.' These instructions strike modern readers as cold-blooded — and they were. We need to read them in their ancient context: David is preserving justice that he himself was politically constrained from executing. The chapter is not endorsing vengeance; it is portraying realpolitik at the threshold of a dynastic transition. Solomon will need a clean political slate to begin his reign; David is identifying the cases that must be settled. We can hear the deathbed clearly: an old king arranging the ledger before he goes.

So David slept with his fathers, and was buried in the city of David. And the days that David reigned over Israel were forty years: seven years reigned he in Hebron, and thirty and three years reigned he in Jerusalem. Then sat Solomon upon the throne of David his father; and his kingdom was established greatly. Guidance: David's death. 'Slept with his fathers' (vayyishkav David im-avotav) — the standard Old Testament idiom for the dignified death of a king. He is gathered to the ancestors who preceded him. 'Buried in the city of David' — Jerusalem; specifically, somewhere on the southeast ridge near the original Davidic citadel. (The exact tomb-location is not known archaeologically; later Jewish tradition placed it on Mount Zion further west, but that identification is post-biblical.) The chronological summary: forty years total — seven in Hebron over Judah only (during the war with the house of Saul), thirty-three in Jerusalem over all twelve tribes. The forty-year reign matches the standard Old Testament generation. Then: 'Solomon sat upon the throne of David his father; and his kingdom was established greatly.' Hebrew vatikkon malkhuto me'od — was firmly fixed exceedingly. The throne is no longer contested. The chapter is briefly stating what the rest of the chapter will dramatize: Solomon's kingdom would be firmly established in the next few months as he settled the open political files David had left behind. The narrator does not eulogize David. Whatever needed to be said about David's life is said in the previous twenty-four chapters of 2 Samuel; here the focus moves to Solomon.

And Adonijah the son of Haggith came to Bathsheba the mother of Solomon. And she said, Comest thou peaceably? And he said, Peaceably. He said moreover, I have somewhat to say unto thee. And she said, Say on. And he said, Thou knowest that the kingdom was mine, and that all Israel set their faces on me, that I should reign: howbeit the kingdom is turned about, and is become my brother's: for it was from the LORD. And now I ask one petition of thee, deny me not. And she said unto him, Say on. And he said, Speak, I pray thee, unto Solomon the king, (for he will not say thee nay,) that he give me Abishag the Shunammite to wife. And Bathsheba said, Well; I will speak for thee unto the king. Guidance: Adonijah makes a fatal move. After his probationary pardon in chapter 1, he comes to Bathsheba — David's widow, Solomon's mother — to ask a favor through her. Bathsheba's first question is wary: 'Comest thou peaceably?' (ha-shalom bo'ekha — is your coming in peace?). He answers 'Peaceably' (shalom). His opening speech is dangerous: 'Thou knowest that the kingdom was mine, and that all Israel set their faces on me, that I should reign.' Adonijah is still nursing the claim. He acknowledges that the LORD has redirected the kingdom — 'for it was from the LORD' — but the past-tense claim 'the kingdom was mine' reveals that he has not really let go. Then the request: 'Give me Abishag the Shunammite to wife.' This sounds, on its surface, like a simple personal favor. But in the ancient world, taking a king's woman was a politically loaded act. Abishag had shared David's bed (even though not sexually — chapter 1 was careful about this) and is treated as part of the harem. To possess her was to make a claim on the previous king's status. Absalom had done the same with David's concubines on the palace roof (2 Sam 16). The act of marriage to a royal woman was an act of royal usurpation. Bathsheba therefore went unto king Solomon, to speak unto him for Adonijah. And the king rose up to meet her, and bowed himself unto her, and sat down on his throne, and caused a seat to be set for the king's mother; and she sat on his right hand. Then she said, I desire one small petition of thee; I pray thee, say me not nay. And the king said unto her, Ask on, my mother: for I will not say thee nay. And she said, Let Abishag the Shunammite be given to Adonijah thy brother to wife. And king Solomon answered and said unto his mother, And why dost thou ask Abishag the Shunammite for Adonijah? ask for him the kingdom also; for he is mine elder brother; even for him, and for Abiathar the priest, and for Joab the son of Zeruiah. Then king Solomon sware by the LORD, saying, God do so to me, and more also, if Adonijah have not spoken this word against his own life. Now therefore, as the LORD liveth, which hath established me, and set me on the throne of David my father, and who hath made me an house, as he promised, Adonijah shall be put to death this day. And king Solomon sent by the hand of Benaiah the son of Jehoiada; and he fell upon him that he died. Guidance: The court audience and Solomon's verdict. Bathsheba enters; Solomon rises to meet her — a striking gesture of filial respect. He bows; sets her a throne 'on his right hand' (the position of honor). The chapter is recording the establishment of the queen-mother (gebirah) role in the Davidic court. The mother is given an institutional dignity that will continue through the dynasty. Bathsheba presents the petition. Solomon's reply is immediate and incandescent. 'Why dost thou ask Abishag the Shunammite for Adonijah? ask for him the kingdom also.' He decodes the request instantly. Adonijah, Abiathar, and Joab — the three he names — are the original Adonijah faction; the request for Abishag would have been the public symbol around which they could regroup. Solomon's oath: 'God do so to me, and more also, if Adonijah have not spoken this word against his own life.' The Adonijah pardon-on-probation condition has been violated. Adonijah has shown 'wickedness' (the chapter 1 condition); he forfeits his life. 'Adonijah shall be put to death this day.' Benaiah is dispatched and the sentence is carried out. The chapter is showing us Solomon as a ruler with sharp political instincts. A coup-survivor must read political symbols quickly; Solomon reads this one instantly. The chapter is also showing the limits of probationary mercy. Adonijah was given a chance; he used the chance to plot. The mercy was real; the consequence-when-mercy-was-spurned was also real.

The chapter is also showing the limits of probationary mercy. Adonijah was given a chance; he used the chance to plot. The mercy was real; the consequence-when-mercy-was-spurned was also real.

And unto Abiathar the priest said the king, Get thee to Anathoth, unto thine own fields; for thou art worthy of death: but I will not at this time put thee to death, because thou barest the ark of the Lord GOD before David my father, and because thou hast been afflicted in all wherein my father was afflicted. So Solomon thrust out Abiathar from being priest unto the LORD; that he might fulfil the word of the LORD, which he spake concerning the house of Eli in Shiloh. Guidance: The deposition of Abiathar. Solomon's mercy is not absent — Abiathar, the priest of David's wilderness years, the man who carried the ephod and gave David the LORD's guidance through the Saul-rebellion (1 Sam 22-23), is not killed. 'I will not at this time put thee to death.' Solomon honors the long service. But the priest who joined Adonijah's coup cannot continue as senior priest in Solomon's court. He is exiled to Anathoth (his hometown, a Levitical city about three miles north-northeast of Jerusalem in Benjamin — later famous as Jeremiah's birthplace, Jer 1:1). The narrator adds the theological comment: 'that he might fulfil the word of the LORD, which he spake concerning the house of Eli in Shiloh.' Abiathar was descended from Eli through Ahimelech. Back in 1 Sam 2:30-36, the LORD had spoken judgment on Eli's house — saying the priestly line would be cut off and a faithful priest raised up in its place. The judgment had hung over Eli's descendants for generations. Solomon's deposition of Abiathar finally executes the long-prophesied transfer of high-priestly office from Eli's line (through Abiathar) to Zadok's line (a separate Aaronic descent). The chapter is showing us how slowly but inexorably the LORD's word works through history. A judgment uttered to Eli around 1100 B.C. is fulfilled around 970 B.C. — over a century later, but fulfilled. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's word never goes unfulfilled, even when its working takes generations.

Then tidings came to Joab: for Joab had turned after Adonijah, though he turned not after Absalom. And Joab fled unto the tabernacle of the LORD, and caught hold on the horns of the altar. And it was told king Solomon that Joab was fled unto the tabernacle of the LORD; and, behold, he is by the altar. Then Solomon sent Benaiah the son of Jehoiada, saying, Go, fall upon him. And Benaiah came to the tabernacle of the LORD, and said unto him, Thus saith the king, Come forth. And he said, Nay; but I will die here. And Benaiah brought the king word again, saying, Thus said Joab, and thus he answered me. And the king said unto him, Do as he hath said, and fall upon him, and bury him; that thou mayest take away the innocent blood, which Joab shed, from me, and from my house. And the LORD shall return his blood upon his own head, who fell upon two men more righteous and better than he, and slew them with the sword, my father David not knowing thereof, to wit, Abner the son of Ner, captain of the host of Israel, and Amasa the son of Jether, captain of the host of Judah. Their blood shall therefore return upon the head of Joab, and upon the head of his seed for ever: but upon David, and upon his seed, and upon his house, and upon his throne, shall there be peace for ever from the LORD. So Benaiah the son of Jehoiada went up, and fell upon him, and slew him: and he was buried in his own house in the wilderness. And the king put Benaiah the son of Jehoiada in his room over the host: and Zadok the priest did the king put in the room of Abiathar. Guidance: Joab's death. When Joab hears that Adonijah is executed and Abiathar exiled, he reads the writing on the wall. He flees to the altar — the same sanctuary Adonijah had used in chapter 1. But there is a crucial difference. Adonijah came as a frightened prince seeking probationary mercy; Joab comes as a convicted murderer of two named victims. The altar offered asylum to the manslayer, not to the deliberate murderer (Exod 21:14). Benaiah goes; orders Joab to come out; Joab refuses: 'Nay; but I will die here.' He chooses to make his death a sacrilege — perhaps hoping the priestly sanctuary would protect him, perhaps embracing a defiant end. Benaiah hesitates and returns to consult Solomon. Solomon's order: 'Do as he hath said, and fall upon him.' The king authorizes the execution at the altar. He gives the theological rationale: 'that thou mayest take away the innocent blood, which Joab shed, from me, and from my house.' The two named victims — Abner (commander of Israel's army, killed peace-time) and Amasa (commander of Judah's army, killed peace-time) — were both 'more righteous and better' than their killer. Their blood pollutes Joab's head; with his execution, the pollution is lifted from David's house. The Davidic dynasty receives the blessing: 'upon David, and upon his seed, and upon his house, and upon his throne, shall there be peace for ever from the LORD.' Joab is killed at the altar, buried in his own house in the wilderness. Benaiah is promoted to commander of the army; Zadok is promoted to high priest. The chapter is showing us a thorough institutional turnover. Solomon's reign begins with the personnel of David's reign settled: the loyalist faction now holds the senior offices; the Adonijah faction is dead, deposed, or exiled. The kingdom's institutional spine has been replaced.

Solomon's reign begins with the personnel of David's reign settled: the loyalist faction now holds the senior offices; the Adonijah faction is dead, deposed, or exiled. The kingdom's institutional spine has been replaced.

And the king sent and called for Shimei, and said unto him, Build thee an house in Jerusalem, and dwell there, and go not forth thence any whither. And Shimei said unto the king, The saying is good: as my lord the king hath said, so will thy servant do. And Shimei dwelt in Jerusalem many days. And it came to pass at the end of three years, that two of the servants of Shimei ran away unto Achish son of Maachah king of Gath. And they told Shimei, saying, Behold, thy servants be in Gath. And Shimei arose, and saddled his ass, and went to Gath to Achish to seek his servants: and Shimei went, and brought his servants from Gath. And it was told Solomon that Shimei had gone from Jerusalem to Gath, and was come again. And the king sent and called for Shimei, and said unto him, Did I not make thee to swear by the LORD, and protested unto thee, saying, Know for a certain, on the day thou goest out, and walkest abroad any whither, that thou shalt surely die? and thou saidst unto me, The word that I have heard is good. Why then hast thou not kept the oath of the LORD, and the commandment that I have charged thee with? And it came to pass at the end of three years, that two of the servants of Shimei ran away unto Achish son of Maachah king of Gath. And they told Shimei, saying, Behold, thy servants be in Gath. And Shimei arose, and saddled his ass, and went to Gath to Achish to seek his servants: and Shimei went, and brought his servants from Gath. And it was told Solomon that Shimei had gone from Jerusalem to Gath, and was come again. And the king sent and called for Shimei, and said unto him, Did I not make thee to swear by the LORD, and protested unto thee, saying, Know for a certain, on the day thou goest out, and walkest abroad any whither, that thou shalt surely die? and thou saidst unto me, The word that I have heard is good.
